explanation:
The input states that if the SelfTest fails, the controller must not proceed to ventilation. The atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' captures the SelfTest failing, and 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation is proceeding. The correct LTL property should enforce that it is never possible to have both a SelfTest failure and ventilation occurring at any time. Thus, the 'globally' (G) operator is used for an invariant: if a SelfTest failure occurs, then 'ventilating' must not occur. The best template is one where 'globally, if selfTestFail then not ventilating', corresponding to 'G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3))'.

- chosen_template_ID: 98
- bool_exp1: selfTestFail
- bool_exp3: ventilating
- N_DURATION: not needed for this template.

The property expresses that whenever the SelfTest fails (indicated by selfTestFail), then it must always be the case that ventilation does not occur (i.e., ventilating is never true after selfTestFail). This prevents the controller from entering the ventilation mode if the SelfTest has failed, capturing the requirement that failing the SelfTest disables ventilation.

The requirement states: If the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation. In this context, 'selfTestFail' captures when the SelfTest procedure fails, and 'ventilating' captures when the controller is ventilating (i.e., proceeded to ventilation). The requirement is expressing a safety property: whenever a self test failure happens (selfTestFail), then ventilation should never occur (i.e., not ventilating). The best way to capture this in LTL is with a global invariant that whenever selfTestFail becomes true, from that point forward, ventilating is never true. The template that fits is G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)), which ensures that after a SelfTest fails, the system never allows ventilation. Thus, bool_exp1=selfTestFail, bool_exp3=(!ventilating).

The requirement states that if the self-test fails, the controller should not be able to proceed to ventilation. In the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestFail' indicates a SelfTest failure and 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation is occurring. The correct translation must guarantee that whenever a SelfTest failure occurs, ventilation should not start (i.e., the system must not enter the ventilation state at any future point after the failure).

This safety property should hold globally: at all times, if the self-test fails, then it must always be the case that 'ventilating' does not hold (i.e., is false; the controller does not begin ventilation). This is best captured by the LTL template: G (selfTestFail -> (G (!ventilating))).

This asserts that globally, whenever 'selfTestFail' becomes true, from that point onwards 'ventilating' is never allowed; this most precisely meets the requirement.

The requirement states: If SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation. The atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' indicates the SelfTest has failed, and 'ventilating' indicates the controller is ventilating. The correct formalization is that if 'selfTestFail' holds, then 'ventilating' must not occur. The template that captures 'Always, if selfTestFail, then not ventilating' is: 'G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3))', with bool_exp1 = selfTestFail, bool_exp3 = ventilating. This ensures that whenever the SelfTest fails, the system does not proceed to ventilation at any future time.

The requirement states that if the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation. The atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' is true when the SelfTest fails, and 'ventilating' is true when the controller is ventilating (i.e., proceeding to ventilation). Thus, whenever 'selfTestFail' holds, 'ventilating' must never hold. This is expressed by the formula G (selfTestFail -> (! ventilating)), which means "It is always the case that if SelfTest fails (selfTestFail), then the controller never enters ventilation (ventilating is false)." This directly captures the requirement's intent.

The requirement states that if the SelfTest fails (atomic proposition: selfTestFail), the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation (atomic proposition: ventilating should NOT be true). In Linear Temporal Logic, this is most accurately captured by choosing a template that ensures it is always the case (G) that if selfTestFail becomes true, then ventilating can never become true. This is reflected by template 38, which expresses 'G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))': globally, if condition bool_exp1 holds (here, selfTestFail), then it is always the case that bool_exp3 (here, ventilating) does not hold. This captures the prohibition of entering ventilation after (or when) SelfTest fails, matching the intended behavior.

The requirement states: If SelfTest fails (indicated by 'selfTestFail'), then the controller must not be able to proceed to ventilation (represented by '!ventilating'). This is best captured by the LTL template 'G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating))', which means "Globally, whenever selfTestFail is true, from that point onward, ventilating is always false". In other words, once the self-test fails, it is required that the system never enters the ventilation state after that. This correctly enforces that a failed self-test permanently blocks proceeding to ventilation.

The requirement states: 'If the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation.' This means that whenever selfTestFail holds (the SelfTest fails), the system must guarantee that ventilating never becomes true. To capture this behavior, we use the LTL formula 'G(selfTestFail -> (G (! ventilating)))', which states: it is always the case that if a SelfTest failure occurs, it will always remain true that the system does not proceed to ventilation. This ensures that the system cannot ventilate after a test failure, as required.

The requirement is that if the SelfTest fails, the controller must not proceed to ventilation. In terms of the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestFail' indicates the SelfTest has failed, and 'ventilating' indicates ventilation is ongoing. We want to express that whenever a SelfTest failure occurs, it must always be the case that ventilation is not entered (i.e., 'ventilating' is false). This is captured by the LTL template G (bool_exp1 -> (!bool_exp3)), where bool_exp1 = selfTestFail and bool_exp3 = ventilating. This means: globally, whenever a SelfTest fails, it is always forbidden for ventilation to proceed.
